title: UC2 — A new mission-control replica won't land on the launch pad
description: Today's mission for the Artemis pad shift: bring up a replacement mission-control replica on the launch pad, then hand the diagnosis to artemis-launch-pad-debugger from the operational CLI.

steps.title: Mission setup — bring up the replacement replica
steps.explanation:
Mission control needs a replacement `mission-control` replica on the Artemis launch pad — the previous one rotated out at end of shift. Apply the bundle below to your vCluster: a namespace, a service, the new replica deployment, and the on-call debugger agent that'll be addressable from the operational CLI when you need it. Once the applies complete, the replacement should be coming up on the cluster.

Four files are dropped into your workspace by this step (`fileEdits`): three manifests under `uc2/manifests/` and one Agent CRD under `uc2/agents/`. The two `kubectl apply -f` commands below send them to the cluster in order.

fileEdits.content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
    kagent-workshop/scenario: pod-pending

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: mission-control
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    app: mission-control
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: mission-control
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: mission-control
  namespace: artemis-uc2
  labels:
    app: mission-control
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mission-control
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: mission-control
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: mission-control
          image: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/mission-control:v1.0.0
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          env:
            - name: APP_IDENTITY
              value: mission-control
          # The friction: `requests.cpu: "1000"` — 1000 cores. No AKS node
          # SKU comes close (the largest M-series caps around 200 cores), so
          # the kube-scheduler can't satisfy it and the Pod stays Pending
          # with `FailedScheduling: 0/N nodes are available: N Insufficient
          # cpu`. The launch-pad-debugger agent diagnoses this in one
          # describe-pod call (the FailedScheduling event surfaces the
          # mismatch).
          #
          # Switched from the prior taint-based mechanism (UC2 originally
          # used a synthetic node taint applied by a bootstrap Job) because
          # vCluster pod-sync topology doesn't propagate node taints to the
          # host scheduler — the host scheduler binds pods against the AKS
          # nodes that don't carry the taint, so the Pod scheduled and ran.
          # Resource requests work at any cluster scale because they sync up
          # to the host alongside the Pod, and the host scheduler evaluates
          # them against actual node capacities.
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "1000"
              memory: "64Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "1000"
              memory: "128Mi"

# UC2 diagnostic agent — diagnoses Pod Pending caused by an unsatisfiable
# CPU resource request on `manifests/20-deployment.yaml`.
#
# Tool surface (tight — describe-pod's inline events name the cause):
#   - kubectl get pod / kubectl describe pod
#
# kagent v0.9.0 sources tools from MCP servers, not a `Tool` CRD (the CRD does
# not exist in v0.9.0; cf. STORY-005 finding, mirrored from UC1). The
# `kagent-tool-server` RemoteMCPServer ships with kagent's demo profile and
# exposes the kubectl read tools used here. Tool names below are the
# kagent-native names — same surface UC1 + UC3 already use.
#
# Lives in the `kagent` namespace (cluster-scope-discoverable, `artemis-`
# prefix per docs/artemis-naming.md L60) — same precedent as UC1 + UC3, and
# a hard prerequisite for UC4's coordinator (STORY-025) which delegates to
# this Agent via kagent's same-namespace a2a wiring.
apiVersion: kagent.dev/v1alpha2
kind: Agent
metadata:
  name: artemis-launch-pad-debugger
  namespace: kagent
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc2
spec:
  type: Declarative
  description: |
    Diagnoses why the Artemis `mission-control` Deployment is not landing
    on the cluster in the `artemis-uc2` namespace. Correlates evidence
    across Pod state, node state, and recent events.
  declarative:
    modelConfig: default-model-config
    systemMessage: |
      You are an Artemis launch-pad on-call SRE. Your job is to diagnose why
      the `mission-control` Deployment in the `artemis-uc2` namespace cannot
      land any Pod on the cluster, and report a single sentence of root cause
      plus a one-line remediation hint.

      Use ONLY the kubectl read tools provided. Do not attempt to apply, patch,
      or delete any resource.

      Diagnostic loop:

        1. `k8s_get_resources` with kind=Pod and namespace=artemis-uc2 — list
           the pods in the namespace. Pick the `mission-control-…` pod.
        2. `k8s_describe_resource` with kind=Pod, namespace=artemis-uc2, and
           the pod name from step 1. Read the phase + conditions + the inline
           events at the bottom. A Pending Pod with a `FailedScheduling`
           event reading `0/N nodes are available: N Insufficient cpu` is
           your smoking gun — the container's `resources.requests.cpu`
           exceeds what any node can provide.

      Surface the exact `requests.cpu` value (visible in describe-pod's
      container spec — usually under `Containers > … > Requests`) and the
      node-side shortfall (from the FailedScheduling event), then recommend
      lowering the CPU request to a realistic value (e.g. `100m` or `1`) as
      the remediation. Mention `kubectl patch deployment/mission-control -n
      artemis-uc2 --type=json -p '[{"op":"replace","path":"/spec/template/spec/containers/0/resources/requests/cpu","value":"100m"}]'`
      (or `kubectl edit` for an interactive fix).

      Be concise. The participant is running through a 5-minute tour and
      needs the answer, not a tutorial.

      Tool budget: at most 1 `k8s_get_resources` + 1 `k8s_describe_resource`
      call. Do NOT call any other tool (node-level inspection is not needed —
      the FailedScheduling event itself names "Insufficient cpu" and pulls
      in the requested value). Do NOT call describe-resource more than once.
      If describe-resource shows the Pod is NOT Pending (e.g. `Running`),
      report that state verbatim and stop — do not loop trying to find a
      Pending pod that doesn't exist.
    tools:
      - type: McpServer
        mcpServer:
          apiGroup: kagent.dev
          kind: RemoteMCPServer
          name: kagent-tool-server
          namespace: kagent
          toolNames:
            - k8s_get_resources
            - k8s_describe_resource

steps.title: Mission status check — is the replacement live?
steps.explanation: The replacement should be online by now. Check that the replica pod has come up. Give the kubelet ~30 s to settle before running the command — if the pod is not Running, you'll see it here, and that's our cue to call in help.

steps.title: Call the agent for help
steps.explanation:
The replica is stuck and the pad shift starts soon. Hand the diagnosis to **`artemis-launch-pad-debugger`** from your terminal — kagent is addressable from the operational CLI you already use, in addition to the dashboard chat surface.

The command below invokes the agent directly and prints its synthesis to your terminal. The exact prompt the agent receives is also surfaced here so you can copy-paste-modify if you want to re-run with a different question:

> The mission-control pod in the artemis-uc2 namespace is stuck Pending. Diagnose it.

The agent has the same Kubernetes read tools you would have run by hand (`describe pod` is enough — the FailedScheduling event surfaces inline). It runs in the `kagent` namespace (where every Artemis debugger lives) and uses the cluster-wide `default-model-config` `ModelConfig`.

commands.label: Ask the agent
commands.command: kagent invoke --agent artemis-launch-pad-debugger --namespace kagent --task 'The mission-control pod in the artemis-uc2 namespace is stuck Pending. Diagnose it.'

steps.title: What we'd have done by hand
steps.explanation:
Without the agent, you would have run two `kubectl` commands and read the inline scheduler event yourself:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n artemis-uc2 -l app=mission-control
kubectl describe pod -n artemis-uc2 -l app=mission-control
```

The `describe pod` output has everything — at the bottom under `Events`, the kube-scheduler logs `FailedScheduling: 0/N nodes are available: N Insufficient cpu`. Up at the top, the container's `Requests:` section shows `cpu: 1000` — the deployment is asking for 1000 CPU cores, which no node in the cluster can satisfy.

The agent ran the same single `describe pod` tool and returned one sentence of root cause: **the container's `requests.cpu` is `1000`, the kube-scheduler can't find a node that big; lower it to a realistic value (e.g. `100m`)**. You ran one command (`kubectl get pods`) just to see the Pod was Pending — you didn't have to read the long describe-pod output to find the FailedScheduling event yourself, and you didn't have to scroll up to cross-reference the Requests block.

Multiplied across the dozen incidents an on-call rotation handles in a week, that's the value the workshop is selling.
